en.extract:
The northern rockhopper penguin (Eudyptes moseleyi), also known as Moseley's rockhopper penguin, Moseley's penguin, or Tristan penguin, is a penguin species  native to the southern Indian and Atlantic Oceans. It was formerly considered conspecific with the eastern and western rockhopper penguins.
A study published in 2009 showed that the population of the northern rockhopper had declined by 90% since the 1950s. For this reason, the northern rockhopper penguin is classified as endangered.


Taxonomy

The rockhopper penguins have been considered to consist of two species, northern and southern rockhopper penguin, since research published in 2006 demonstrated morphological, vocal, and genetic differences between the two populations. The latter species was itself later split into the eastern rockhopper penguin and the western rockhopper penguin. Molecular datings suggest that the genetic divergence between the northern and the two southern species of rockhopper penguin may have been caused by a vicariant event caused by a shift in the position of the Subtropical Front during the mid-Pleistocene climate transition.
Analysis of a part of a mitochondrial control region from a northern rockhopper penguin found on the Kerguelen Islands showed that it may have come from Gough Island, 6,000 km away, and that the southern and northern rockhoppers are genetically separate, though some individuals may disperse from their breeding colonies. Northern rockhopper penguin is now widely recognized as a distinct species.


Distribution and habitat
More than 99% of northern rockhoppers breed during late spring or early summer on Tristan da Cunha and Gough Island in the south Atlantic Ocean. Breeding colonies are also found on the French Southern and Antarctic Lands of Amsterdam Island and St Paul Island.
It is very rare for the bird to make landfall on the Australian continent, but in 2006, a northern rockhopper was sighted at Robe, South Australia, and in July 2023 a very underweight northern rockhopper was found at Goolwa beach in South Australia.


Genetic variation and morphology
The rockhopper penguin (Eudyptes chrysocome) was split into three distinct subspecies in 1992; the southern, (E. c. chrysocome), eastern (E. c. filholi) and northern rockhopper penguin (E. c.  moseleyi). The three subspecies are distinguished by differences in the length of the tassels of the crests, the size and colour of the fleshy margin of the gape, colour pattern on the underside of the flipper and differences in the size of the superciliary stripe in front of the eye. Additionally, the northern rockhopper penguin is larger than the other two subspecies. Proof that the three subspecies were truly different, in terms of more than reproductive isolation and some morphological features, was found in the mitochondrial sequences of the three species. It was found E. c. filholi and E. c. chrysocome were not as different genetically as E. c. moseleyi was to the other two subspecies. The level of genetic differentiation was similar to the genetic difference found in other penguin subspecies groups.
Another way that scientists were able to differentiate the different subspecies of rockhoppers were in the parasites that fed off each separate rockhopper penguin species. Penguins are parasitised by 15 species of chewing lice in two genera, Austrogoniodes and the monotypic Nesiotinus. Within rockhopper penguins, the host-specific louse Austrogoniodes keleri is present only on southern rockhoppers; the multi-host Austrogoniodes concii parasitises only northern rockhoppers and Austrogoniodes hamiltoni parasitises only eastern rockhoppers. Cross contamination of the lice is not possible naturally due to the fact that chewing lice have limited mobility and heavily rely on their host for survival, meaning that the lice can only be transmitted across species by way of close physical contact.


Ecology and reproduction


Food and feeding
The northern rockhopper penguin feeds on krill and other sea life such as crustaceans, squid, octopus and fish.


Reproduction
It breeds in colonies in a range of locations from sea level or on cliff sides, to sometimes inland. The Northern form, found in Amsterdam and Gough island, is reproductively isolated from the Crozet and Kerguelen islands. They are monophyletic; meaning they have a split in the mitochondria DNA trees which forms two subspecies: the northern and southern rockhopper penguin. Another interesting difference between the two subspecies is their mating ritual. They both use different songs and head ornaments in their mating signals. The reproductive isolation has led to not only physical difference but also behavioral. The adults feed their chicks lower trophic level prey than they themselves consume. During breeding season the adults eat zooplankton and then transition to fish, showing they favor future reproduction.


Population and threats

The current population is estimated to be between 100,000–499,999 breeding pairs at Gough Island, 18,000 to 27,000 pairs at Inaccessible Island, and 3,200 to 4,500 at Tristan da Cunha. In the Indian Ocean, the population was 25,500 pairs on Amsterdam Island, and 9,000 pairs on St Paul Island in 1993; there has been no information available on population trends there since the 1990s. Atlantic Ocean sites show a decline of 2.7% per year; the drop in the population at Gough Island has been described as equivalent to the loss of 100 birds every day since the 1950s.
A study published in 2009 showed that the world population of the northern rockhopper had declined by 90% since the 1950s, possibly because of climate change, changes in marine ecosystems and overfishing for squid and octopus by humans. Other possible factors in the decline include disturbance and pollution from ecotourism and fishing, egg-harvesting, and predation and competition from subantarctic fur seals (Arctophoca tropicalis). Surveys show that the birds are at risk of infection by goose barnacles. House mice (Mus musculus) have been introduced into their environment by human sea expeditions. The mice have proven to be invasive, and consume northern rockhopper eggs, as well as hunt their young. In order to preserve the birds, a culling of the mice is being considered. The climate change conditions alter food availability since it reduces nutrients and productivity. This causes an effect on the body mass, causing a negative impact on their reproductivity success.
The northern rockhopper penguin is classified as endangered because of the decline in numbers over the last three generations (or 30 years).
Population Trends by Year and Island


2011 oil spill
On March 16, 2011, the Maltese-registered freighter MS Oliva ran aground on Nightingale Island, spilling tons of heavy crude into the ocean. The crew was rescued, but the ship broke up, leaving an oil slick that surrounded the island, threatening its population of rockhopper penguins. Nightingale Island has no fresh water, so the penguins were transported to Tristan da Cunha for cleaning.


In culture
Cody Maverick, a northern rockhopper penguin, the main protagonist in the 2007 film Surf's Up and its sequel Surf's Up 2: WaveMania.
In the Happy Feet and Happy Feet Two films, the Barry White-like love guru, Lovelace, is a rockhopper penguin, who will instil his wisdom for the price of rocks. Lovelace is voiced by Robin Williams.


References


External links

The '''northern rockhopper penguin''' ('''''Eudyptes moseleyi'''''), also known as '''Moseley's rockhopper penguin''', '''Moseley's penguin''', or '''Tristan penguin''', is a [[penguin]] species  native to the southern [[Indian Ocean|Indian]] and [[Atlantic Ocean]]s. It was formerly considered [[conspecific]] with the [[eastern rockhopper penguin|eastern]] and [[western rockhopper penguin]]s.

A study published in 2009 showed that the population of the northern rockhopper had declined by 90% since the 1950s. For this reason, the northern rockhopper penguin is classified as [[Endangered species|endangered]].

==Taxonomy==
[[File:Rockhopper penguin.jpg|thumb|Rockhopper penguin skeleton in [[Manchester Museum]]]]
The rockhopper penguins have been considered to consist of two species, northern and [[southern rockhopper penguin]], since research published in 2006 demonstrated [[morphology (biology)|morphological]], vocal, and genetic differences between the two populations.<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal|doi = 10.1111/j.1365-294X.2006.03028.x|title = Genetic isolation and divergence in sexual traits: Evidence for the northern rockhopper penguin Eudyptes moseleyi being a sibling species|year = 2006|last1 = Jouventin|first1 = P.|last2 = Cuthbert|first2 = R. J.|last3 = Ottvall|first3 = R.|journal = Molecular Ecology|volume = 15|issue = 11|pages = 3413–3423|pmid = 16968279| bibcode=2006MolEc..15.3413J |s2cid = 12724253}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|doi=10.1007/s00300-006-0160-3|title=Genetic evidence for three species of rockhopper penguins, Eudyptes chrysocome|year=2006|last1=Banks|first1=Jonathan|last2=Van Buren|first2=Amy|last3=Cherel|first3=Yves|last4=Whitfield|first4=James B.|journal=Polar Biology|volume=30|issue=1 |pages=61–67|bibcode=2006PoBio..30...61B |s2cid=6087437}}</ref> The latter species was itself later split into the [[eastern rockhopper penguin]] and the [[western rockhopper penguin]]. [[molecular clock|Molecular datings]] suggest that the genetic divergence between the northern and the two southern species of rockhopper penguin may have been caused by a [[vicariance|vicariant]] event caused by a shift in the position of the [[Subtropical Front]] during the [[Mid-Pleistocene Transition|mid-Pleistocene climate transition]].<ref>{{Cite journal|doi = 10.1111/j.1365-2699.2008.02014.x|title = Speciation chronology of rockhopper penguins inferred from molecular, geological and palaeoceanographic data|year = 2009|last1 = De Dinechin|first1 = Marc|last2 = Ottvall|first2 = Richard|last3 = Quillfeldt|first3 = Petra|last4 = Jouventin|first4 = Pierre|journal = Journal of Biogeography|volume = 36|issue = 4|pages = 693–702| bibcode=2009JBiog..36..693D | s2cid=86520290 }}</ref>
Analysis of a part of a [[D-loop|mitochondrial control region]] from a northern rockhopper penguin found on the [[Kerguelen Islands]] showed that it may have come from Gough Island, 6,000 km away, and that the southern and northern rockhoppers are genetically separate, though some individuals may disperse from their breeding colonies.<ref>{{Cite journal|doi=10.1007/s00300-007-0350-7|title=A northern rockhopper penguin unveils dispersion pathways in the Southern Ocean|year=2007|last1=De Dinechin|first1=Marc|last2=Pincemy|first2=Gwenaëlle|last3=Jouventin|first3=Pierre|journal=Polar Biology|volume=31|issue=1 |pages=113–115|bibcode=2007PoBio..31..113D |s2cid=43366621}}</ref> Northern rockhopper penguin is now widely recognized as a distinct species.

==Distribution and habitat==
More than 99% of northern rockhoppers breed during late spring or early summer on [[Tristan da Cunha]] and [[Gough Island]] in the south [[Atlantic Ocean]].<ref name="factsheet">[http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/index.html?action=SpcHTMDetails.asp&sid=32304&m=0 BirdLife International (2008). Species factsheet: ''Eudyptes moseleyi'']. Retrieved 16 January 2009.</ref> Breeding colonies are also found on the [[French Southern and Antarctic Lands]] of [[Île Amsterdam|Amsterdam Island]] and [[Île Saint-Paul|St Paul Island]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Northern Rockhopper Penguin|url=https://abcbirds.org/bird/northern-rockhopper-penguin/|access-date=2020-10-11|website=American Bird Conservancy|language=en}}</ref>

It is very rare for the bird to make landfall on the Australian continent, but in 2006, a northern rockhopper was sighted at [[Robe, South Australia]], and in July 2023 a very underweight northern rockhopper was found at [[Goolwa, South Australia|Goolwa beach]] in [[South Australia]].<ref>{{cite web | last=Horn | first=Caroline | title=Northern rockhopper penguin found on Goolwa Beach, SA | website=[[ABC News (Australia)]] | date=25 July 2023 | url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-07-25/northern-rockhopper-penguin-found-on-goolwa-beach-sa/102643566 | access-date=27 July 2023}}</ref>

== Genetic variation and morphology ==
The rockhopper penguin (''Eudyptes chrysocome)'' was split into three distinct subspecies in 1992; the southern, (''E. c. chrysocome''), eastern (''E. c. filholi'') and northern rockhopper penguin (''E. c.  moseleyi''). The three subspecies are distinguished by differences in the length of the tassels of the crests, the size and colour of the fleshy margin of the gape, colour pattern on the underside of the flipper and differences in the size of the superciliary stripe in front of the eye. Additionally, the northern rockhopper penguin is larger than the other two subspecies.<ref>{{Cite book|title = Handbook of Australian, New Zealand, and Antarctic Birds.|last = Marchant S|first = Higgins PJ|publisher = Oxford University Press|year = 1990|location = Oxford}}</ref> Proof that the three subspecies were truly different, in terms of more than reproductive isolation and some morphological features, was found in the mitochondrial sequences of the three species. It was found ''E. c. filholi'' and ''E. c. chrysocome'' were not as different genetically as ''E. c. moseleyi'' was to the other two subspecies. The level of genetic differentiation was similar to the genetic difference found in other penguin subspecies groups.<ref name=":1" />

Another way that scientists were able to differentiate the different subspecies of rockhoppers were in the parasites that fed off each separate rockhopper penguin species. Penguins are parasitised by 15 species of chewing lice in two genera, ''Austrogoniodes'' and the monotypic ''Nesiotinus''. Within rockhopper penguins, the host-specific louse ''Austrogoniodes keleri'' is present only on southern rockhoppers; the multi-host ''Austrogoniodes concii'' parasitises only northern rockhoppers and ''Austrogoniodes hamiltoni'' parasitises only eastern rockhoppers.<ref>{{Cite book|title = World Checklist of Chewing Lice with Host Associations and Keys to Families and Genera|author = Price|author2 = Hellenthal|author3 = Palma|publisher = Illinois Natural History Survey Special Publication 24|year = 2003|location = Champaign, IL|pages = 1–448}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title = Louse-host associations {{!}} Phthiraptera.info|url = http://phthiraptera.info/host-lice?louse=&host=&order=name_1&sort=asc|website = phthiraptera.info|access-date = 2015-10-30}}</ref> Cross contamination of the lice is not possible naturally due to the fact that chewing lice have limited mobility and heavily rely on their host for survival, meaning that the lice can only be transmitted across species by way of close physical contact.<ref>{{Cite book|title = The Ecology of Ectoparasitic Insects|last = Marshall|first = AG|publisher = Academic|year = 1981|location = London}}</ref>

==Ecology and reproduction==

===Food and feeding===
The northern rockhopper penguin feeds on [[krill]] and other sea life such as [[crustacean]]s, [[squid]], [[octopus]] and [[fish]].<ref name="factsheet"/>

===Reproduction===
It breeds in [[Bird colony|colonies]] in a range of locations from sea level or on cliff sides, to sometimes inland.<ref name="factsheet"/> The Northern form, found in Amsterdam and Gough island, is reproductively isolated from the Crozet and Kerguelen islands. They are monophyletic; meaning they have a split in the mitochondria DNA trees which forms two subspecies: the northern and southern rockhopper penguin.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = The breeding biology and factors affecting reproductive success in rockhopper penguins Eudyptes chrysocome at Macquarie Island|journal = Polar Biology|date = 2004-07-22|issn = 0722-4060|pages = 711–720|volume = 27|issue = 11|doi = 10.1007/s00300-004-0643-z|first1 = Cindy L.|last1 = Hull|first2 = Mark|last2 = Hindell|first3 = Kirsten Le|last3 = Mar|first4 = Paul|last4 = Scofield|first5 = Jane|last5 = Wilson|first6 = Mary-Anne|last6 = Lea| bibcode=2004PoBio..27..711H |s2cid = 1883713}}</ref> Another interesting difference between the two subspecies is their mating ritual. They both use different songs and head ornaments in their mating signals. The reproductive isolation has led to not only physical difference but also behavioral.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Genetic isolation and divergence in sexual traits: evidence for the northern rockhopper penguin Eudyptes moseleyi being a sibling species|journal = Molecular Ecology|date = 2006-10-01|issn = 0962-1083|pmid = 16968279|pages = 3413–3423|volume = 15|issue = 11|doi = 10.1111/j.1365-294X.2006.03028.x|first1 = P.|last1 = Jouventin|first2 = R. J.|last2 = Cuthbert|first3 = R.|last3 = Ottvall| bibcode=2006MolEc..15.3413J |s2cid = 12724253}}</ref> The adults feed their chicks lower trophic level prey than they themselves consume. During breeding season the adults eat zooplankton and then transition to fish, showing they favor future reproduction.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Higher trophic level prey does not represent a higher quality diet in a threatened seabird: implications for relating population dynamics to diet shifts inferred from stable isotopes|journal = Marine Biology|volume = 161|issue = 10|pages = 2243–2255|doi = 10.1007/s00227-014-2502-y|year = 2014|last1 = Morrison|first1 = Kyle W.|last2 = Bury|first2 = Sarah J.|last3 = Thompson|first3 = David R.| bibcode=2014MarBi.161.2243M |s2cid = 83630787}}</ref>

===Population and threats===
[[File:Zuchterfolge Felsenpinguine.jpg|thumb|Northern rockhopper penguins in [[Tiergarten Schönbrunn]]]]
[[File:Rockhopper inaccessible.jpg|right|thumb|Northern rockhopper penguins on [[Inaccessible Island]], drawn by the naturalist aboard [[HMS Challenger (1858)|HMS ''Challenger'']]]]
The current population is estimated to be between 100,000–499,999 breeding pairs at Gough Island, 18,000 to 27,000 pairs at Inaccessible Island, and 3,200 to 4,500 at Tristan da Cunha. In the Indian Ocean, the population was 25,500 pairs on Amsterdam Island, and 9,000 pairs on St Paul Island in 1993; there has been no information available on population trends there since the 1990s. Atlantic Ocean sites show a decline of 2.7% per year;<ref name="factsheet"/> the drop in the population at Gough Island has been described as equivalent to the loss of 100 birds every day since the 1950s.<ref>[https://www.nbcnews.com/id/wbna28696101 nbcnews.com. ''Northern rockhopper penguins near extinction'']. 16 January 2009. Retrieved 18 March 2009.</ref>

A study published in 2009 showed that the world population of the northern rockhopper had declined by 90% since the 1950s, possibly because of [[climate change]], changes in [[marine ecosystem]]s and [[overfishing]] for squid and octopus by humans.<ref>[http://www.birdlife.org/news/news/2009/01/rockhopper_decline.html BirdLife International. ''Penguins are walking an increasingly rocky road'']. 16 January 2009. Retrieved 16 January 2009.</ref> Other possible factors in the decline include disturbance and pollution from [[ecotourism]] and fishing, egg-harvesting, and predation and competition from [[subantarctic fur seal]]s (''Arctophoca tropicalis'').<ref name="factsheet" /> Surveys show that the birds are at risk of infection by [[goose barnacle]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Goose barnacles on seals and a penguin at Gough Island|last = Reisinger|first = Ryan|date = March 3, 2010|journal = African Zoology|volume = 45|pages = 129–132|doi = 10.1080/15627020.2010.11657262|hdl = 2263/14281|s2cid = 219289034|hdl-access = free}}</ref> [[House mouse|House mice]] (''Mus musculus'') have been introduced into their environment by human sea expeditions. The mice have proven to be invasive, and consume northern rockhopper eggs, as well as hunt their young. In order to preserve the birds, a culling of the mice is being considered.<ref>{{Cite journal|title = Predation of Atlantic Petrel chicks by house mice on Gough Island|journal = Animal Conservation|date = 2012-10-01|issn = 1469-1795|pages = 472–479|volume = 15|issue = 5|doi = 10.1111/j.1469-1795.2012.00534.x|first1 = R. M.|last1 = Wanless|first2 = N.|last2 = Ratcliffe|first3 = A.|last3 = Angel|first4 = B. C.|last4 = Bowie|first5 = K.|last5 = Cita|first6 = G. M.|last6 = Hilton|first7 = P.|last7 = Kritzinger|first8 = P. G.|last8 = Ryan|first9 = M.|last9 = Slabber| bibcode=2012AnCon..15..472W | s2cid=83773363 }}</ref> The climate change conditions alter food availability since it reduces nutrients and productivity. This causes an effect on the body mass, causing a negative impact on their reproductivity success.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Delord|first1=Karine|title= Factors affecting adult body condition in the endangered northern rockhopper penguin|last2=Cotté|first2=Cédric|last3=Terray|first3=Pascal|last4=Bost|first4=Charles‑André|last5=Weimerskirch|first5=Henri|last6=Barbraud|first6=Christophe|journal=Marine Biology|year=2021|volume=168|issue=3|page=27 |doi=10.1007/s00227-021-03832-z|bibcode=2021MarBi.168...27D |s2cid=233948224|issn=0025-3162|url=https://hal.archives-ouvertes.fr/hal-03381813/file/Delord%20factores%20affecting%20adult%20body%20composition%20Marine%20Biol%202021.pdf }}</ref>

The northern rockhopper penguin is classified as endangered because of the decline in numbers over the last three generations (or 30 years).<ref name="factsheet"/>

===2011 oil spill===
On March 16, 2011, the Maltese-registered freighter [[MS Oliva|MS ''Oliva'']] ran aground on [[Nightingale Island]], spilling tons of heavy crude into the ocean. The crew was rescued, but the ship broke up, leaving an oil slick that surrounded the island, threatening its population of rockhopper penguins.<ref>{{cite web|title=MS Oliva runs aground on Nightingale Island |publisher=The Tristan da Cunha Website |url=http://www.tristandc.com/newsmsoliva.php|access-date=23 March 2011}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|title= Oil Spill Menaces Penguins |doi=10.1126/science.331.6024.1499-b |journal=Science|pages= 1499–1500 |volume= 331 |issue=6024 |date= 25 March 2011}}</ref> Nightingale Island has no fresh water, so the penguins were transported to [[Tristan da Cunha]] for cleaning.<ref>[https://news.bbc.co.uk/earth/hi/earth_news/newsid_9438000/9438609.stm BBC News] Oil-soaked rockhopper penguins in rehabilitation</ref>

== In culture ==
* Cody Maverick, a northern rockhopper penguin, the main protagonist in the 2007 film ''[[Surf's Up (film)|Surf's Up]]'' and its sequel ''[[Surf's Up 2: WaveMania]]''.<ref>[https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0423294/ Surf's Up at IMDb]</ref>
* In the ''[[Happy Feet]]'' and ''[[Happy Feet Two]]'' films, the [[Barry White]]-like love guru, Lovelace, is a rockhopper penguin, who will instil his wisdom for the price of rocks. Lovelace is voiced by [[Robin Williams]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Happy Feet at IMDb|url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0366548/|website=IMDb|date=17 November 2006|access-date=26 October 2015}}</ref>

zh.extract:
西凤头黄眉企鹅（台湾作西方長冠企鵝，学名：Eudyptes moseleyi），是企鹅目企鹅科的一种鸟类。
西凤头黄眉企鹅体重约2327.85克，翼长约102.2毫米，嘴峰长约53.4毫米，喙宽度约14毫米，喙厚度约20.4毫米，跗蹠长约34.8毫米，尾长约62.4毫米。西凤头黄眉企鹅是部分迁徙的鸟类，其栖息在开放的海洋及海岛环境中，食性为肉食性，主要食物来源是水生动物。
西凤头黄眉企鹅分布于特里斯坦-达库尼亚、戈夫岛、圣保罗和阿姆斯特丹岛。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。


参考资料
